name: Hydrogen
Analysis:
Period 1 tells the simplest—and most dramatic—electron gain enthalpy story. Hydrogen readily gains an electron, while helium strongly resists it.

Hydrogen has a negative electron gain enthalpy because accepting an electron completes its 1s² configuration, releasing energy and forming the stable hydride ion (H⁻).

Helium, however, already has a completely filled 1s² shell. Any additional electron must enter the much higher-energy 2s orbital, requiring a huge input of energy. As a result, helium has an exceptionally large positive electron gain enthalpy.

The dramatic jump from hydrogen to helium highlights the extraordinary stability of a completely filled noble-gas shell—making helium one of the least willing atoms to accept an extra electron.

name: Lithium
Analysis:
Period 2 is full of surprises, with electron gain enthalpy swinging between highly favorable and highly unfavorable values. The trend is controlled not just by nuclear charge, but also by subshell stability and electron–electron repulsion.

Lithium readily gains an electron to complete its 2s subshell, giving a moderately negative value. Beryllium, however, has a large positive electron gain enthalpy because the incoming electron must enter the higher-energy 2p orbital beyond the stable 2s² configuration.

Carbon becomes much more favorable because gaining an electron creates the stable half-filled 2p³ configuration. Nitrogen then breaks the trend—its half-filled 2p³ subshell is already exceptionally stable, so adding another electron forces pairing and becomes energetically unfavorable, giving a positive electron gain enthalpy.

Oxygen returns to a negative value as the stronger nuclear attraction outweighs electron–electron repulsion, while fluorine has the most negative electron gain enthalpy in the period due to its strong drive to complete the 2p⁶ noble-gas configuration.

Neon ends the period with another twist. Its filled 2s²2p⁶ shell makes electron gain highly unfavorable, as an additional electron would have to occupy the much higher-energy 3s orbital, so reliable experimental values are generally unavailable.

name: Sodium
Analysis:
Period 3 shows a clear increase in the tendency to gain an electron—but with some fascinating exceptions. As we move from sodium (Na) to chlorine (Cl), electron gain enthalpy generally becomes more negative because the effective nuclear charge increases. However, subshell stability creates several interesting deviations.

Sodium readily gains an electron to complete its 3s subshell, while magnesium has an electron gain enthalpy close to zero because the incoming electron must enter the higher-energy 3p orbital beyond its stable 3s² configuration.

Silicon shows a sharp increase in negativity because gaining an electron forms the stable half-filled 3p³ configuration. Phosphorus then breaks the trend—its 3p³ subshell is already exceptionally stable, so adding another electron causes pairing and reduces stability.

Sulfur becomes much more favorable as the increasing nuclear attraction outweighs the repulsion between paired electrons, and chlorine reaches the most negative electron gain enthalpy in the period by completing the stable 3p⁶ noble-gas configuration.

Argon ends the period with the biggest surprise. Its filled 3s²3p⁶ shell is so stable that an additional electron must enter the much higher-energy 4s orbital, giving it a very large positive electron gain enthalpy.

name: Potassium
Analysis:
Period 4 follows the familiar trend of becoming more favorable for electron gain—but the transition metals add several twists. From potassium (K) to bromine (Br), electron gain enthalpy generally becomes more negative as the effective nuclear charge increases, although subshell stability creates several interesting irregularities.

Potassium readily gains an electron to complete its 4s subshell, whereas calcium has an electron gain enthalpy close to zero because the incoming electron must enter the higher-energy 3d orbital. Scandium gains slight stabilization as the extra electron occupies a 3d orbital.

In the p-block, gallium shows only a moderately negative value, but germanium displays a sharp increase because gaining an electron forms the stable half-filled 4p³ configuration. Arsenic then breaks the trend—its 4p³ subshell is already exceptionally stable, so electron pairing makes electron gain less favorable.

Selenium becomes more favorable again as the increasing nuclear attraction outweighs pairing repulsion, and bromine reaches the most negative electron gain enthalpy in the period by completing the stable 4p⁶ noble-gas configuration.

name: Rubidium
Analysis:
Period 5 follows the familiar trend of becoming more favorable for electron gain—but several electronic configurations create fascinating twists. From rubidium (Rb) to iodine (I), electron gain enthalpy generally becomes more negative as the effective nuclear charge increases.

Rubidium readily gains an electron to complete its 5s subshell, while strontium has a value close to zero because the incoming electron must enter the higher-energy 4d orbital. Yttrium gains slight stabilization as the added electron forms a 4d² configuration.

In the p-block, indium shows only a moderately negative value, but tin displays a sharp increase because gaining an electron forms the stable half-filled 5p³ configuration. Antimony then breaks the trend—its 5p³ subshell is already exceptionally stable, so electron pairing makes electron gain slightly less favorable.

Tellurium again becomes more favorable as the increasing nuclear attraction overcomes pairing repulsion, while iodine has a highly negative electron gain enthalpy because gaining one electron completes its 5p⁶ noble-gas configuration.

Xenon ends the period with a surprise. Its filled valence shell makes electron gain slightly unfavorable, as an additional electron must occupy the higher-energy 6s orbital, giving it a small positive electron gain enthalpy.

name: Cesium
Analysis:
Period 6 generally becomes more favorable for electron gain from cesium (Cs) to astatine (At), but the trend is shaped by several fascinating exceptions. Increasing effective nuclear charge makes the electron gain enthalpy progressively more negative, while lanthanide contraction adds an extra layer of complexity.

Cesium readily gains an electron to complete its 6s subshell, whereas barium shows a much less negative value because the incoming electron must enter a higher-energy orbital beyond the stable 6s² configuration. Hafnium has a slightly negative electron gain enthalpy (−1.3 kJ mol⁻¹), indicating that electron gain is only weakly favorable. Although the added electron forms the more stable 5d3 configuration, the stabilization gained is largely offset by the effects of lanthanide contraction, changes in 5d orbital energies, and electron correlation.

In the p-block, thallium gains an electron to form a 6p² configuration, while lead forms the stable half-filled 6p³ configuration. However, the large 6p orbitals limit the stabilization achieved. From lead to bismuth and polonium, the electron gain enthalpy becomes increasingly more negative as the rising effective nuclear charge outweighs the effects of electron pairing.

Astatine reaches the most negative electron gain enthalpy in the period, reflecting its strong tendency to gain one electron and move toward the stable radon-like configuration.

name: Francium
Analysis:
Period 7 is the least explored region of the periodic table. Most of its elements are highly radioactive, exist for only fractions of a second, and are produced atom-by-atom, making reliable electron gain enthalpy measurements extremely challenging.

Among the known values, francium (Fr) has a moderately negative electron gain enthalpy because the added electron completes its 7s subshell. Radium (Ra) is much less favorable, as the incoming electron must occupy a higher-energy orbital beyond the stable 7s² configuration.

The actinides show moderately negative electron gain enthalpies, with the added electron entering the 5f orbitals, where exchange energy, actinide contraction, and shielding effects all influence the trend.

For the transactinides and superheavy p-block elements—from rutherfordium (Rf) to oganesson (Og)—experimental data are largely unavailable. Theoretical studies predict that strong relativistic effects dramatically reshape the 7s, 7p, 6d, and 5f orbitals, causing their electron gain behavior to deviate from the familiar periodic trends observed in lighter elements.
